sound_type: guttural croak in flight -- harsh, low-pitched, croaking contact call. Not musical. Functional. E=4.
ecosystem: River -- clear, large rivers with abundant fish populations. PNW rivers: Skagit, Snoqualmie, Skykomish, Stillaguamish. Also large lakes in winter. Requires clean water, fish, riparian forest.
season: Year-round resident in the Pacific Northwest. May move to lakes when river conditions deteriorate in winter.
family: Anatidae (second entry, after Harlequin Duck at degree 118)

coordinates.description: Reference for river habitat in the Cascade foothills. Common Mergansers found on clear rivers throughout the PNW.

taxonomy_note.description: Second Anatidae entry (after Harlequin Duck at 118). Largest merganser in North America. Fish-eating specialist with serrated bill (sawbill). The Anatidae streak: 118-119 and counting. River ecosystem. Indicator of healthy rivers.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Anatidae count: 2 entries (Harlequin Duck 118, Common Merganser 119). Streak: 2 consecutive.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Mergus from Latin mergus -- 'diving bird' or 'diver.' merganser from Latin mergus + anser (goose) -- 'diving goose.' Common Merganser: the common diving goose.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Mergus -- the merganser genus. Three North American species: Common, Red-breasted, Hooded. Sawbill ducks with serrated bills for fish-catching.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Anseriformes continues (second consecutive). The waterfowl order. After Piciformes (woodpeckers) and Passeriformes (dipper), Anseriformes has entered with consecutive entries.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Anatidae second entry. Streak begins at 118. Continues at 119. Track continuation at 120.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 500-2,500 Hz. Low, harsh call. Most energy in low frequencies.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 800-1,500 Hz. The guttural croak concentrates energy in the low-frequency range.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Croak: 500-2,500 Hz. Male courtship call (uig-a): softer, bell-like, higher-pitched.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual croak: 0.3-0.8 seconds. Given in series during flight. Short, functional calls.
song_metrics.note_structure: GUTTURAL CROAK: Harsh, hoarse, low-pitched croaking call. Given in flight. Contact calling. Not musical. Female croak harsher than male courtship call.
song_metrics.note_types: Croak (primary, contact/alarm), male courtship call (softer uig-a), alarm calls. Relatively silent while foraging.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Individual croaks in loose series. No complex phrasing. Functional communication.
song_metrics.timbre: Harsh. Guttural. Croaking. Not musical. A functional, raspy quality. Low-pitched for a duck.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=4 -- moderate. Audible across river corridors but not dominating. The merganser is primarily a visual presence.
song_metrics.tempo: Irregular. Croaks given in loose series during flight or disturbance. No rhythmic pattern.
song_metrics.context: CONTACT AND ALARM. Given in flight, during disturbance, during courtship. Relatively silent while foraging underwater.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal documented variation. Functional calls tend to be less variable than learned songs.
song_metrics.key_property: GUTTURAL CROAK IN FLIGHT -- harsh, low-pitched, functional. E=4. Second Anatidae entry. Largest merganser. Sawbill adaptation: serrated bill for fish-catching. River ecosystem indicator. Cooperative group fishing. The Anatidae streak continues.

plumage.overall: Male: bright white body, dark green-black head with shaggy crest, long red-orange serrated bill. Striking. Female: gray body, warm rufous-brown crested head, same red-orange serrated bill. Large, streamlined duck.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 119: (1) LONG RED-ORANGE SERRATED BILL -- the sawbill. Narrow, hooked, unlike any dabbling duck. (2) MALE: white body, dark green head. (3) FEMALE: gray body, rufous head with crest. (4) LARGE SIZE -- biggest merganser. (5) STREAMLINED PROFILE on water.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Males in eclipse plumage resemble females. Otherwise consistent year-round.
plumage.flight_pattern: Direct, fast flight with rapid wingbeats. Low over water. White wing patches visible in flight. Often in small flocks following river corridors.

physical_description.overall: Large duck. Body length 58-72 cm. Wingspan 82-97 cm. Weight 0.9-2.1 kg. Streamlined body. Set-back legs. Long, narrow, serrated bill.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 119: large duck with long red-orange serrated bill gliding on clear river. Male: white body, dark green head. Female: gray body, rufous crested head. If the bill is long, narrow, and serrated -- it is a merganser.
physical_description.field_id: Large, streamlined duck on clear rivers. Long red-orange bill with serrated edges. Male: white body, dark green-black head. Female: gray body, rufous head. Dives for fish. Often in small flocks. The sawbill.

nodes.title: The Sawbill -- Serrated Bill Adaptation
nodes.description: Backward-pointing tomia along both mandibles. Hooked tip. Narrow, cylindrical shape. Catches fish up to 30 cm. A one-way trap: fish can slide backward toward swallowing but not forward toward escape. The defining merganser adaptation.

nodes.title: Anatidae Streak -- Waterfowl Entrance (118-119+)
nodes.description: Second consecutive Anatidae entry. Harlequin Duck (118) to Common Merganser (119). The engine enters the largest waterfowl family. From forest birds to water birds. Track continuation.

nodes.title: The Guttural Croak -- Functional Call
nodes.description: Harsh, low-pitched croak in flight. 500-2,500 Hz. Contact calling. Not musical. The merganser is a visual presence, not an acoustic one. E=4.

nodes.title: River Ecosystem Indicator
nodes.description: Presence requires clean water, abundant fish, riparian forest. The merganser signals river health. PNW rivers: Skagit, Snoqualmie, Skykomish, Stillaguamish.

nodes.title: Water Continuum -- Upstream to Downstream (116-119)
nodes.description: Dipper (116-117, fast stream) to Harlequin (118, turbulent stream) to Merganser (119, river). Following the water downstream. Getting wider, deeper, calmer. Birds getting larger. Foraging shifting from invertebrates to fish.

nodes.title: Cooperative Group Fishing
nodes.description: Flocks of 10-50+ swim in coordinated lines driving fish into shallows. Remarkable cooperative hunting strategy for a duck species. Resembles strategies of cormorants and pelicans.

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE ANATIDAE. Harlequin Duck (118, turbulent stream, E=5) to Common Merganser (119, river, E=4). Anatidae streak continues. Water calms. Energy descends.
WATER CONTINUUM. Dipper: fast mountain stream, songbird (E=5). Merganser: river, duck (E=4). Water theme continues downstream. Invertebrate forager to fish hunter.
UNDERWATER FORAGING. Both forage underwater. Dipper walks on stream bottoms (60g). Merganser dives and pursues fish (1.7kg). Different scales, same medium.
CAVITY NESTER. Both cavity nesters. Flicker excavates. Merganser uses existing large cavities in riparian trees. Woodpecker cavities enable duck nesting.
INDICATOR SPECIES. Black-backed: fire forest health indicator. Merganser: river ecosystem health indicator. Both signal ecosystem integrity in their domains.
